text.title: Competitive Analysis (UX) Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Competitive Analysis (UX) application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The competitor set MUST include 3-5 direct + 2-3 indirect + 1-2 aspirational competitors. Single-bucket analyses are rejected.
rule.rationale: Direct competitors set the baseline; indirect reveal alternative approaches; aspirational reveal best-in-class targets.

rule.statement: Evaluation criteria MUST be declared before any competitor is examined. Post-hoc criterion addition is rejected.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc criteria become rationalisation; pre-declaration keeps the analysis honest.

rule.statement: Every finding MUST be classified Must | Should | Could | Avoid. Loose findings without classification are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without MSCW the analysis is documentation, not a decision artefact.

rule.statement: Each competitor profile MUST be based on first-hand product use, not marketing copy. Profiles assembled from websites only are rejected.
rule.rationale: Marketing copy hides the actual UX; first-hand use surfaces the real friction.

rule.statement: Each finding MUST cite a screenshot or session-recording reference. Uncited claims are rejected.
rule.rationale: Citations make the analysis falsifiable; uncited claims are opinion.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
